name: Alexandre SEPULVEDA de DIETRICH
headline: Data Scientist and Business Analytics MS candidate
targetRoleLine: Seeking Summer 2026 Data Science, AI Engineering, and ML internships in NYC
about: I build practical data and AI systems: anomaly detection for finance teams, optimization models for public-policy and healthcare operations problems, and product-grade ML tools with modern web stacks.
heroProofBullets: Built an accounting data quality and anomaly detection pipeline at CHANEL Europe.
Won the ElevenLabs voice AI track in NYC and built AI-lexandre, a recruiter-facing interview assistant with grounded citations and live streaming.
impactHighlights: Built production-grade data pipelines and anomaly detection workflows for finance teams at CHANEL Europe
Shipped AI product MVPs and portfolio-native agent experiences with FastAPI, Next.js, and LLM orchestration
Built optimization and simulation models for childcare access, hospital planning, and healthcare appointment dynamics

projects.title: AI-lexandre
projects.summary: Recruiter-facing interview assistant that answers in first person from my published portfolio, streams responses live, and shows source-backed evidence.
projects.result: Built a GitHub Pages frontend plus a Railway-ready Fastify API with role-aware retrieval, streaming responses, and grounded citations over my own project corpus.
highlights: Turns the portfolio into an interactive interview product instead of a static list of project cards.
Builds a local corpus from portfolio projects, case studies, experience, education, and skills, then retrieves role-relevant evidence before answering.
Streams answers live in the UI while keeping links back to the source projects and writeups visible.

learning.role: Designed and built the portfolio-native interview assistant, from content corpus and retrieval logic to the static chat UI and Railway-ready API.
decisions: Kept content.json as the source of truth so the public portfolio and interview assistant stay aligned.
Separated the GitHub Pages frontend from the Fastify API so the static site stays simple while the answer runtime can evolve independently.
Filtered visible citations to sources actually referenced in the final answer.
tradeoffs: Accepted more orchestration around retrieval, prompting, streaming, and citations to reduce unsupported answers.
Kept the first version as a lightweight local corpus instead of adding a database before the evidence model was stable.
failures: Early answers could sound confident while relying on thin or mismatched evidence.
Short follow-up questions initially caused repeated project summaries instead of a new answer angle.
lessons: A portfolio AI assistant needs evidence design as much as prompt design.
Source visibility makes failure modes easier to debug because the user can see what the answer used.
Follow-up handling needs explicit memory and retrieval context, not just a chat transcript.
learning.evidenceNotes: Public portfolio artifacts are available; details are limited to material that can be shared openly.
nextImprovements: Add a richer learning memory layer across every project and experience.
Use recent conversation summary during retrieval for vague follow-up questions.

facts: The system is a static GitHub Pages frontend plus a Railway-hosted Fastify API.
The API exposes health, config, evidence search, full response, and streaming response endpoints.
The evidence layer is generated from content.json and includes projects, case studies, experience, education, skills, and learning profiles.
Answers use role-aware retrieval, OpenAI generation, streaming UI updates, and citation filtering so visible sources match the answer.

projects.title: Tomorrow You
projects.summary: Hackathon-winning agent-based decision support system that turns structured user interviews into personalized future scenarios and interactive conversations with simulated future selves.
projects.result: Won Best Use of Voice AI in NYC on the ElevenLabs track and shipped an end-to-end FastAPI + Next.js prototype with multi-step reasoning and voice interaction pipelines.
highlights: Built an end-to-end agent workflow that generates future scenarios from structured interviews and supports conversations with simulated future selves.
Designed multi-step reasoning and voice interaction pipelines with Mistral AI, ElevenLabs, FastAPI, and Next.js.
Public demo is temporarily offline during migration and API reroute; code and architecture write-up remain available.

learning.role: Built the end-to-end hackathon prototype across structured intake, scenario generation, voice interaction, and web/API integration.
decisions: Structured the experience as stages from interview intake to generated future scenarios to interactive future-self conversations.
Used Mistral AI and ElevenLabs with FastAPI and Next.js to make the prototype demonstrable within the hackathon window.
Prioritized an end-to-end user flow over a narrow model experiment.
tradeoffs: Chose a more orchestrated workflow instead of relying on one broad prompt so each stage could be inspected and improved.
Accepted prototype-level reliability constraints to ship a complete live experience quickly.
failures: Multi-step AI flows can lose coherence if intermediate outputs are not structured enough.
Voice interaction adds latency and state-management failure modes on top of the reasoning pipeline.
lessons: A convincing AI product prototype depends on interaction design as much as model choice.
Structured intake is the best way to keep personalized generation grounded.
Hackathon scope forces clear decisions about which reliability issues to solve now and which to document for migration.
learning.evidenceNotes: Public portfolio artifacts are available; details are limited to material that can be shared openly.
nextImprovements: Complete the demo migration and API reroute.
Add more explicit evaluation prompts for scenario coherence and user safety.

facts: The product flow starts with a neutral onboarding interview, then builds a structured user profile and current-self card.
It generates future-self branches and supports conversation with a selected simulated future self.
Runtime components are organized around routers, engines, models, storage, and providers.
The backend API surface includes onboarding, pipeline orchestration, future generation, and conversation routes.
The data model tracks userProfile, currentSelf, futureSelvesFull, explorationPaths, transcript, and memory nodes.

projects.title: HelpfulLens
projects.summary: Yelp review helpfulness modeling pipeline with sentiment-enriched features, reproducible data prep, and exploratory analysis helpers.
projects.result: Built an end-to-end Python workflow from raw Yelp dumps through feature engineering, EDA, and modeling artifacts.
highlights: Built ingest and cleaning steps for Yelp review data without heavy platform dependencies.
Added sentiment-enriched feature engineering with lightweight and transformer-based options.
Packaged the work as a reproducible applied-ML repo with reports and notebooks.

learning.role: Built the applied-ML pipeline for Yelp review helpfulness modeling, including cleaning, feature engineering, exploratory analysis, and report artifacts.
decisions: Kept data preparation reproducible so modeling experiments could be traced back to cleaned inputs.
Combined lightweight sentiment features with optional transformer-based enrichment.
Packaged the project as an applied-ML repo with notebooks and reports rather than only a final model.
tradeoffs: Balanced feature richness with reproducibility and dependency simplicity.
Used the project to show modeling workflow discipline more than production deployment.
failures: Raw review data required careful cleaning before model comparisons were meaningful.
Text features can improve signal while also making the pipeline heavier to reproduce.
lessons: Applied ML work is only credible when data preparation is explicit.
EDA and feature documentation make model results easier to interpret later.
A smaller reproducible workflow is more useful than a complex notebook that cannot be rerun.
learning.evidenceNotes: Public portfolio artifacts are available; details are limited to material that can be shared openly.
nextImprovements: Add clearer model comparison tables and validation notes.
Package the pipeline with a smaller sample dataset for easier reproduction.

facts: The repo converts Yelp JSON dumps into parquet caches and cleaned tables through src/data and pipeline scripts.
Feature engineering supports numeric features, city/state/category one-hots, optional TF-IDF, and optional sentiment merges.
Sentiment enrichment can use VADER or DistilBERT, including a compact DistilBERT subset workflow.
Modeling includes baselines, Poisson, tree regressor, and optional hurdle model with saved metrics, plots, models, and predictions.
The pipeline can run ingest, clean, dataset assembly, feature building, and train/evaluate end to end.

projects.title: Childcare Deserts NYC
projects.summary: Optimization project for reducing NYC childcare deserts by combining facility expansions with new-site planning under cost and spacing constraints.
projects.result: Built a seven-stage notebook pipeline with ideal and realistic planning scenarios and exported report-ready tables and figures.
highlights: Formulated the planning problem as a staged optimization workflow with shared cleaning and parameter estimation.
Compared benchmark and realistic deployment scenarios using structured notebook stages.
Produced report artifacts grounded in exported solution tables, figures, and solver logs.

learning.role: Formulated and implemented the optimization workflow for evaluating childcare expansion and new-site planning scenarios in NYC.
decisions: Split the work into staged notebooks so cleaning, parameter estimation, benchmark scenarios, and realistic scenarios stayed auditable.
Compared idealized and realistic planning assumptions instead of presenting a single optimization answer.
Exported report-ready tables and figures from the solver workflow.
tradeoffs: Used a structured notebook pipeline for transparency rather than hiding the workflow behind a polished app.
Balanced mathematical formulation with policy interpretability for non-technical readers.
failures: Spacing, budget, and facility-expansion constraints can change which solutions look good.
An idealized optimum can be misleading unless realistic deployment limits are modeled.
lessons: Optimization projects need scenario design, not just solver output.
Policy-oriented models should make assumptions visible because assumptions drive recommendations.
Exported artifacts help turn a notebook into a defensible report.
learning.evidenceNotes: Public portfolio artifacts are available; details are limited to material that can be shared openly.
nextImprovements: Add sensitivity analysis for cost and spacing assumptions.
Build a lightweight map view for solution comparison.

facts: The project is structured as a seven-stage notebook workflow for data preparation, benchmark planning, and realistic planning scenarios.
The optimization combines facility expansion decisions with new-site planning under cost and spacing constraints.
The public evidence is strongest for formulation, scenario comparison, exported tables, exported figures, and solver logs.
The project is best used to discuss policy modeling assumptions rather than a deployed application.

projects.title: Codebase Analyzer
projects.summary: WIP developer tool that ingests a repository and produces architecture diagrams, structure reports, onboarding explanations, and starter tasks.
projects.result: Current MVP spans a Fastify API and lightweight web UI for local or GitHub repo inputs.
highlights: Targets a real onboarding problem: helping engineers understand unfamiliar codebases quickly.
Combines repo crawling, artifact generation, and a lightweight review UI in one monorepo.
Strong supporting evidence for developer tooling and AI-assisted onboarding workflows.

learning.role: Built the current MVP structure for a developer onboarding tool, including repo ingestion, API shape, generated artifacts, and a lightweight UI.
decisions: Focused the MVP on concrete onboarding outputs: architecture diagrams, structure reports, explanations, and starter tasks.
Used a Fastify API plus web UI so local and GitHub repository inputs can share one workflow.
Kept the product framed around developer comprehension instead of generic code summarization.
tradeoffs: Kept the first version broad enough to explore user value before over-optimizing analysis depth.
Accepted WIP status while preserving clear docs and product framing.
failures: Codebase analysis can become noisy if outputs are not tied to developer tasks.
A repository crawler needs guardrails so generated explanations do not overstate architectural certainty.
lessons: Developer AI tools need output contracts that map to real onboarding actions.
A useful analysis product should explain confidence and limitations, not just produce diagrams.
Repo structure alone is not enough; the tool needs task-oriented interpretation.
learning.evidenceNotes: Public portfolio artifacts are available; details are limited to material that can be shared openly.
nextImprovements: Add confidence levels for generated architecture claims.
Add regression fixtures for known repository shapes.

facts: The target user is a new engineer, tech lead, or anyone picking up an unfamiliar repository quickly.
Core MVP outputs are an Excalidraw architecture map, long markdown structure report, podcast-style explanation, starter tasks, and best-effort who/why signals.
Inputs include a local repo path or GitHub URL, with future zip upload and private clone support listed as enhancements.
Explicit non-goals include perfect call graph/runtime tracing, full why reconstruction without docs or commits, and security-hardened multi-tenant SaaS.
Future enhancements include in-browser Excalidraw rendering, git-blame ownership, ADR detection, focused LLM file picking, streaming progress logs, and TTS audio.

projects.title: CUIMC Appointment Scheduling Research
projects.summary: Research project with Columbia University Irving Medical Center under Prof. Carri Chan on delay-sensitive appointment scheduling, cancellations, no-shows, and policy tradeoffs.
projects.result: Built a reusable simulation package, formal modeling note, and policy-comparison notebooks for healthcare scheduling research.
highlights: Developed a rolling-horizon simulator for delay-sensitive booking, cancellation, and no-show behavior.
Compared FCFS, reserved-capacity, and booking-window policies in a healthcare operations setting.
Research project with Prof. Carri Chan at CUIMC, with calibration to Columbia Doctors workflows and data planned.

learning.role: Built healthcare scheduling research artifacts around simulation, policy comparison, and reusable modeling notes under Prof. Carri Chan.
decisions: Modeled delay-sensitive appointment dynamics with cancellations and no-shows instead of treating scheduling as static capacity allocation.
Compared FCFS, reserved-capacity, and booking-window policies to expose operational tradeoffs.
Built reusable simulation and notebook artifacts to support research iteration.
tradeoffs: Used simulation to capture operational dynamics that would be hard to express in a single closed-form model.
Separated current modeling evidence from future calibration work to avoid overclaiming results.
failures: Healthcare scheduling policies can look strong under one metric while hurting another.
Without calibration, simulation results must be framed as research evidence rather than production conclusions.
lessons: Operations research in healthcare requires explicit links between access, utilization, financial metrics, and patient behavior.
Research code is more valuable when it is packaged for repeated policy experiments.
Model credibility depends on stating what is calibrated now and what remains planned.
learning.evidenceNotes: Public portfolio artifacts are available; details are limited to material that can be shared openly.
nextImprovements: Calibrate simulation behavior to Columbia Doctors workflows and available data.
Add clearer experiment tracking for policy comparisons.

facts: The core feedback loop is that longer backlogs create longer delays, delays reduce show-up probabilities, no-shows reduce effective capacity, and reduced capacity can increase backlog further.
The cross-paper synthesis emphasizes delay sensitivity as more important than average no-show level for deciding scheduling policy.
The research compares control levers including panel size, overbooking/service rate, appointment-window limits, and slot salvage through walk-ins or ancillary work.
The models differ in whether the objective is access quality, throughput, net reward, or service level, which changes the preferred policy.
The reusable modeling guidance is to choose the objective first, then decide whether to enrich state with booked slots, cancellation holes, patient class mix, and promised delay.

projects.title: LinkedIn Note Copilot
projects.summary: Chrome extension plus FastAPI backend for generating personalized LinkedIn connection notes from the current profile page.
projects.result: Built a working browser-extension workflow with configurable profile inputs and OpenAI-backed note generation.
highlights: Combines browser automation ergonomics with a clean backend generation service.
Presented as its own project because the shipped workflow is clearer than the umbrella utilities repo.
Strong supporting evidence of practical shipping instincts around workflow automation.

learning.role: Built the browser-extension workflow and backend generation service for personalized LinkedIn connection notes.
decisions: Used a Chrome extension so the workflow lived where the user already reads profiles.
Separated profile capture from note generation through a FastAPI backend.
Made inputs configurable so notes could reflect both the profile and the sender context.
tradeoffs: Optimized for a narrow workflow that could be shipped quickly instead of a broad CRM product.
Accepted a simple backend architecture because the product value was in workflow fit.
failures: Personalized outreach becomes generic if the system does not capture enough profile-specific context.
Browser automation creates UX edge cases around page state and profile parsing.
lessons: Small automation tools are strongest when they remove one repeated task completely.
LLM output quality depends heavily on the input capture UX.
A scoped workflow can be a better portfolio signal than a large but unfinished utility set.
learning.evidenceNotes: Public portfolio artifacts are available; details are limited to material that can be shared openly.
nextImprovements: Add stronger profile parsing validation.
Add saved templates for different networking contexts.

facts: The product is a minimal Chrome extension plus FastAPI backend for drafting LinkedIn connection notes.
The extension works on the currently open LinkedIn profile page and does not perform automatic clicking.
The options page stores sender context such as headline, location, schools, experiences, proof points, focus areas, internship goal, and do-not-say phrases.
The backend exposes a generation route and separates routing, generation orchestration, OpenAI client code, planning, and rendering.
The generation service follows a planning to model call to parsing to validation to logging workflow.

projects.title: Zeit
projects.summary: FastAPI scheduling assistant prototype with a live demo, seeded weekly planning flow, and generated work blocks from tasks plus hard events.
projects.result: Built and deployed an end-to-end planning demo with seeded data, schedule generation, persisted blocks, and tests across API, solver, and UI flows.
highlights: Added a server-rendered demo UI plus JSON endpoints for tasks, events, blocks, demo reset, and schedule generation.
Implemented a weekday scheduler that respects hard events, due dates, and work-window limits while surfacing unscheduled tasks clearly.
Made the project interview-ready with deterministic seed/reset flows, automated tests, and a public Railway deployment.

learning.role: Built and deployed the scheduling assistant prototype across API endpoints, solver workflow, seeded demo state, persistence, and tests.
decisions: Modeled scheduling around hard events, due dates, work windows, and explicit unscheduled-task feedback.
Added seed and reset flows so the public demo stayed deterministic for reviewers.
Kept API and UI flows testable before expanding the scheduling feature set.
tradeoffs: Chose predictable scheduling constraints over a more flexible but harder-to-debug assistant behavior.
Prioritized a deployable demo with known seeded data over full calendar-product scope.
failures: Scheduling tools lose trust quickly if tasks disappear silently or constraints are hidden.
A demo without deterministic reset behavior is hard for reviewers to evaluate consistently.
lessons: Decision-support products need to show infeasibility clearly, not only successful schedules.
Seeded demos make backend projects much easier to evaluate externally.
Optimization logic is stronger when paired with user-facing explanations.
learning.evidenceNotes: Public portfolio artifacts are available; details are limited to material that can be shared openly.
nextImprovements: Add calendar import/export support.
Expose richer explanations for why tasks were left unscheduled.

facts: Zeit is a FastAPI scheduling assistant with a server-rendered demo page, JSON endpoints, SQLAlchemy data model, and Alembic migrations.
The scheduler plans Monday-Friday 9-5 in 30-minute slots, respects hard events and hard due dates, persists planned blocks, and reports unscheduled work.
ScheduleRun records persisted solver diagnostics so placement decisions and raw technical traces can be audited after a run.
The app falls back to a deterministic greedy scheduler when OR-Tools is not usable in the current runtime.
The demo includes reset and seeded data flows so reviewers can return the system to a deterministic baseline.

projects.title: DNA Plasmid Closure with Genetic Algorithms
projects.summary: Evolutionary computation project that learns dinucleotide rotation parameters so 3D DNA trajectories close into plasmid loops.
projects.result: Implemented the genetic algorithm, 3D trajectory model, visualization tooling, and packaged docs/tests.
highlights: Strong evidence of mathematical modeling and scientific computing depth.
Useful portfolio support for optimization and experimental workflow design.
Best presented as coursework or research engineering rather than a product build.

learning.role: Implemented the genetic algorithm, trajectory model, visualization tooling, and packaged documentation for the plasmid closure problem.
decisions: Used evolutionary search to learn dinucleotide rotation parameters that produce closed 3D DNA trajectories.
Paired the optimization routine with visualization so candidate solutions could be inspected geometrically.
Positioned the work as scientific computing and research engineering rather than a product demo.
tradeoffs: Accepted stochastic optimization behavior in exchange for a flexible search process over geometric parameters.
Focused on interpretable modeling artifacts rather than production packaging.
failures: Evolutionary algorithms require careful evaluation because good-looking candidates can still fail closure criteria.
Scientific visualizations need to be tied back to objective values to avoid qualitative overclaiming.
lessons: Optimization work benefits from visual debugging when the objective is geometric.
Scientific computing projects need both mathematical framing and reproducible code.
Coursework projects can still show research-engineering depth when packaged clearly.
learning.evidenceNotes: Public portfolio artifacts are available; details are limited to material that can be shared openly.
nextImprovements: Add more experiment logs for parameter sensitivity.
Document convergence behavior across repeated runs.

facts: The objective is to adapt a 3D DNA trajectory model so a long plasmid sequence closes into a loop.
Fitness combines Euclidean distance between trajectory endpoints and alignment between initial and final direction vectors.
The genetic algorithm uses random perturbation initialization, selection, crossover, and bounded mutation of twist and wedge angles.
The repository includes plasmid_8k and plasmid_180k FASTA sequences and matplotlib trajectory visualization.
The public report explicitly notes that measured results and benchmarks are not included.

caseStudies.title: Chanel Europe Analytics Pipeline
caseStudies.subtitle: Data Scientist Intern, Advanced Analytics and Data Science · CHANEL Europe
caseStudies.summary: Designed a mixed-type outlier detection approach for accounting transactions with hierarchical business categories in an SAP context.
context: Built during an internship in CHANEL Europe's Advanced Analytics and Data Science team.
Project served finance stakeholders who needed stronger trust in SAP-driven accounting data.
Because the underlying production data and workflows are confidential, shared artifacts focus on the method.
problem: Global thresholds were not sufficient because many anomalies were only abnormal within a specific categorical branch.
Some risky transactions had normal-looking amounts but suspicious combinations of categorical attributes.
constraints: Data mixed numeric amounts with high-cardinality hierarchical categorical fields from business systems.
Outputs had to remain interpretable and easy to triage in finance review workflows.
approach: Built two complementary detectors: one for amount anomalies conditioned on transaction context, and one for anomalous categorical combinations.
Used hierarchy-aware categorical representations so parent-child category structure remained informative.
Generated ranked anomaly candidates with clear reason signals to support analyst investigation.
results: Delivered a reusable algorithmic workflow for mixed-type outlier detection in hierarchical accounting data.
Published a technical note summarizing the method: Combination Outlier Detection.

media.alt: Placeholder visual for CHANEL Europe analytics pipeline case study.
media.caption: Supporting method details are available in the Combination Outlier Detection note.

nextImprovements: Add formal model and data drift monitoring dashboards.
Package reusable quality checks for faster rollout across adjacent finance datasets.

learning.role: Led the method design and implementation for a hierarchy-aware anomaly detection workflow during the CHANEL Europe data science internship.
decisions: Separated amount anomalies from categorical-combination anomalies so finance users could inspect different risk signals.
Used hierarchy-aware categorical representations to preserve parent-child business structure.
Designed ranked outputs with interpretable reason signals for analyst review.
tradeoffs: Balanced model sophistication with interpretability because the output needed to fit finance review workflows.
Kept public artifacts method-focused to respect confidentiality while still showing technical depth.
failures: Global thresholds missed anomalies that were only suspicious within a specific business context.
High-cardinality hierarchical categories made naive encoding and distance measures less reliable.
lessons: Business context can be the difference between noise and a useful anomaly signal.
Trustworthy data quality tools need explanations that match how analysts investigate records.
Confidential work can still be portfolio-ready when method, constraints, and evidence boundaries are explicit.
learning.evidenceNotes: Sanitized public description; confidential datasets, internal stakeholders, and proprietary implementation details are intentionally omitted.
nextImprovements: Add formal model and data drift monitoring dashboards.
Package reusable quality checks for faster rollout across adjacent finance datasets.

caseStudies.title: Forvia Multi-Sensor Localization Research
caseStudies.subtitle: Research Project · CentraleSupélec L2S Lab and Forvia
caseStudies.summary: Research project on multi-sensor localization using camera-derived priors and array measurements with an optimal-transport-based fusion approach.
context: Built in a collaboration between CentraleSupélec L2S Lab and Forvia.
Project explored whether lower-cost sensing could support robust localization in controlled conditions.
problem: Single-sensor signals were not sufficiently robust for stable localization on their own.
The team needed a fusion prototype that could combine a camera prior with array-based measurements.
constraints: Sensor alignment and synchronization had to remain stable across test conditions.
Camera priors and array likelihoods had different noise and resolution characteristics.
Prototype scope focused on controlled test-area validation rather than full production deployment.
approach: Built a camera-derived spatial prior from image-processing outputs.
Generated spatial likelihood maps from array measurements and fused them with an optimal-transport-based objective.
Validated localization behavior through controlled experiments and shared findings with partners.
results: Produced a working multi-sensor localization pipeline for controlled experiments.
Authored a research report documenting the setup, method, and findings.

media.alt: Placeholder visual for Forvia multi-sensor localization case study.
media.caption: Research report is available in project artifacts.

nextImprovements: Evaluate robustness under additional lighting and motion conditions.
Add confidence scoring and failure-case analysis for each tracking sequence.

learning.role: Built the fusion prototype and research artifacts for combining camera-derived priors with array measurements in controlled localization experiments.
decisions: Converted camera outputs into a spatial prior that could be fused with array-based likelihood maps.
Used an optimal-transport-based objective to reconcile sensing modalities with different noise and resolution characteristics.
Kept validation focused on controlled experiments rather than claiming production readiness.
tradeoffs: Accepted controlled-scope validation so the research question could be tested cleanly.
Used a mathematically richer fusion method to better represent uncertainty across modalities.
failures: Single-sensor localization was not robust enough on its own.
Misalignment, synchronization, lighting, and motion conditions could degrade the fusion signal.
lessons: Sensor fusion is as much about uncertainty alignment as algorithm choice.
Research prototypes need clear boundaries around test conditions.
Confidence and failure-case analysis should be designed into localization experiments early.
learning.evidenceNotes: Public portfolio artifacts are available; details are limited to material that can be shared openly.
nextImprovements: Evaluate robustness under additional lighting and motion conditions.
Add confidence scoring and failure-case analysis for each tracking sequence.

highlights: Developing a discrete-event simulation of outpatient appointment scheduling to study the link between delays, no-shows, cancellations, and patient access.
Evaluating policies such as FCFS, reserved capacity, booking windows, and overbooking across utilization and financial metrics.

learning.role: Research assistant developing simulation and policy-comparison tools for outpatient appointment scheduling research.
decisions: Modeled appointment scheduling as a dynamic system with delays, no-shows, cancellations, and access effects.
Compared policy families rather than optimizing one isolated metric.
tradeoffs: Used simulation to represent operational behavior while keeping calibration limits explicit.
Balanced utilization, access, and financial metrics instead of treating one as sufficient.
failures: A policy can improve one metric while creating hidden access or workload costs.
Research conclusions can overreach if calibration status is not made explicit.
lessons: Healthcare operations models need to expose tradeoffs between patient access and operational feasibility.
Reusable experiment code is essential for credible policy comparison.
learning.evidenceNotes: Sanitized research description; patient data and internal workflows are not public.
nextImprovements: Add calibrated experiments once data access and workflow assumptions are finalized.
Document policy comparison results with clearer metric dashboards.

highlights: Built a respiratory-system planning component as part of the Nantes University Hospital engagement, alongside emergency patient-flow forecasting and physician scheduling optimization.
Scoping hospital operations tools for emergency patient-flow forecasting and physician scheduling optimization.

Eliciting stakeholder needs and translating operational rules, staffing constraints, and workflow requirements into modeling specifications for a complex scheduling system involving about 80 physicians.
Building decision-support models for patient-flow forecasting and schedule generation under workload, overtime, prioritization, and feasibility constraints.

learning.role: Consultant translating hospital operations requirements into forecasting and scheduling model specifications, including a respiratory-system planning component.
decisions: Started with stakeholder requirements and operational rules before selecting modeling approaches.
Separated emergency patient-flow forecasting, physician scheduling, and respiratory-system planning into connected decision-support components.
Modeled workload, overtime, prioritization, and feasibility constraints explicitly.
tradeoffs: Balanced model ambition with the need for explainable specifications that hospital stakeholders could validate.
Kept the public description high-level to avoid exposing hospital operational details.
failures: Hospital planning models can fail if they ignore staffing rules and workflow constraints.
Ambiguous requirements need structured elicitation before optimization can be trusted.
lessons: Healthcare decision-support work starts with operations discovery, not code.
Stakeholder language must be translated into constraints before a model becomes useful.
Sensitive domains require clear boundaries around what can be shown publicly.
learning.evidenceNotes: Sanitized consulting description; hospital data, internal workflows, and stakeholder details are confidential.
nextImprovements: Validate model specifications against representative operational scenarios.
Build review artifacts that clinicians and planners can critique without reading code.

highlights: Reconciled fragmented commercial, budget, and ERP-style data across 6 countries and about 60 resellers to build a consistent European reporting layer.
Built 5 dashboards standardizing GP, NSV, and volume KPIs, giving negotiation teams a consolidated cross-country view.
Created mapping tables and repeatable transformation logic to align inconsistent identifiers, naming conventions, and local business definitions across sources.

learning.role: Data analyst building cross-country reporting and transformation logic for commercial excellence teams.
decisions: Created mapping tables to reconcile inconsistent identifiers, naming conventions, and business definitions.
Standardized GP, NSV, and volume KPIs across countries and resellers.
Built dashboards around negotiation-team needs rather than only source-system structure.
tradeoffs: Prioritized consistent reporting definitions over preserving every local reporting convention.
Balanced dashboard speed with traceable transformation logic.
failures: Fragmented source data can make simple KPI comparisons misleading.
Dashboard adoption depends on trust in mappings, not just visual polish.
lessons: Data products often start with definitions and reconciliation before modeling.
Cross-country analytics requires explicit handling of local business rules.
Reusable transformation logic is more valuable than one-off reporting fixes.
learning.evidenceNotes: Sanitized public description; confidential datasets, internal stakeholders, and proprietary implementation details are intentionally omitted.
nextImprovements: Add stronger automated checks around mapping completeness.
Document KPI lineage so future dashboards can reuse the reporting layer.

experience.company: CHANEL Europe, Advanced Analytics & Data Science
highlights: Led end-to-end development of CRAFT, an anomaly detection system over 30M+ accounting entries, from business scoping to near-production deployment for corporate finance teams.
Developed explainable anomaly-detection pipelines for hierarchical mixed data, integrating business rules, evolving mappings, and stakeholder-validated review workflows.
Designed clustering and multivariate forecasting methods for 300+ monthly sales series, enabling cluster-level forecasting templates in small-data settings.

learning.role: Data scientist leading anomaly detection, review workflow, and forecasting work for European finance and business analytics use cases.
decisions: Scoped CRAFT end to end from business problem through near-production anomaly-detection workflow.
Integrated business rules, evolving mappings, and stakeholder validation into the modeling pipeline.
Used clustering and multivariate forecasting methods to support template generation in small-data sales settings.
tradeoffs: Balanced technical model depth with explainability needed by finance teams.
Kept portfolio evidence sanitized while preserving the method and decision logic.
failures: Accounting anomaly systems can fail when they ignore hierarchical business context.
Small-data forecasting requires careful grouping because individual series can be too sparse.
lessons: Production-relevant ML requires business scoping, review workflows, and data quality work before modeling pays off.
Explainability is a product requirement when outputs drive finance review.
Confidential projects need explicit evidence boundaries in public storytelling.
learning.evidenceNotes: Sanitized public description; confidential datasets, internal stakeholders, and proprietary implementation details are intentionally omitted.
nextImprovements: Add fuller public method diagrams where confidentiality allows.
Show more sanitized examples of how stakeholder feedback shaped model outputs.
